title: New York Fed Report Shows Households Adding Debt

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed's Household Debt and Credit Report for Q4 2013 showed outstanding household debt increased $241 billion from the previous quarter—the largest quarter-over-quarter increase since Q3 2007. Total household indebtedness rose to $11.52 trillion, still 9.1 percent below the Q3 2008 peak of $12.68 trillion. This marked the first time since before the Great Recession that household debt increased over its year-ago level, signaling an end to the deleveraging period.
key_points: Total household debt increased $241 billion in Q4 2013, the largest quarterly increase since Q3 2007
Total household indebtedness reached $11.52 trillion, still 9.1% below the Q3 2008 peak of $12.68 trillion
Mortgage debt increased $152 billion (1.9%) to $8.05 trillion, accounting for the bulk of the rise
Student loan debt rose $53 billion to $1.08 trillion; auto loan debt increased $18 billion to $863 billion
90+ day delinquency rates: mortgages 3.9%, student loans 11.5%, credit cards 9.5%, all categories 5.0%
New foreclosure notations reached approximately 157,000, down 70% from the Q2 2009 peak of 566,000
why_it_matters: This report marks a turning point in household sector behavior—the first year-over-year increase in consumer borrowing since before the Great Recession. For macro analysis, rising household debt may indicate shifting consumer behavior after an extended deleveraging period. The decline in delinquency rates (overall 90+ day delinquency fell from 5.3% to 5.0%) and foreclosures (to ~157,000 from the 566,000 peak in Q2 2009) reflects improving credit conditions alongside new borrowing activity. Van der Klaauw of the NY Fed characterized this as a significant shift, noting households are 'borrowing again' after a long deleveraging period.
macro_context: Household debt trends are a key input for consumer spending capacity and broader economic growth projections. The Q4 2013 data showing resumption of household borrowing after an extended deleveraging period has implications for Fed policy assessment of household sector health. Student loan debt ($1.08 trillion) and rising mortgage debt ($8.05 trillion) represent structural shifts in household balance sheets. The $241 billion quarterly increase was the largest since Q3 2007, preceding the financial crisis.
important_numbers: $241 billion quarterly increase in household debt
$11.52 trillion total household debt as of Q4 2013
